company_profile.name: Gold Royalty Corp.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Gold Royalty Corp., a precious metals-focused royalty company, provides financing solutions to the metals and mining industry in the United States, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Canada, Brazil, and Mexico. It focuses on acquiring royalties, streams, or similar interests at varying stages of the mine life cycle to build a portfolio offering near, medium, and longer-term returns for its investors. The company was incorporated in 2020 and is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada.
company_profile.description_summary: Gold Royalty Corp., a precious metals-focused royalty company, provides financing solutions to the metals and mining industry in the United States, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Canada, Brazil, and Mexico. It focuses on acquiring royalties, streams, or similar interests at varying stages of the mine life cycle to build a portfolio offering near, medium, and longer-term returns for its investors.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality.note:
Moat score is null — AI Studio adds moat (0-10) and recalculates.
With moat: final_pct = (profitability + financial_strength + growth + predictability + moat) / 50 × 100.
Then re-classify using tiebreaker_inputs:
  ≥70%      → Safe (no penalty)
  65–69%    → Safe if 2+ of (roe≥0.13, roic≥0.13, eps_next_5y≥0.10) else Speculative (−3pts)
  55–64%    → Speculative (−3pts)
  50–54%    → Speculative if 2+ of (roe≥0.08, roic≥0.08, eps_next_5y≥0.08) else Dangerous (−8pts)
  <50%      → Dangerous (−8pts)
Moat override: if moat≥7 AND (profitability≥7 OR fs≥7) AND pct≥55 → Safe.
final_score_pct = max(0, final_pct − penalty)

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Gold Royalty Corp. carries a Dangerous rating because it shows significant weaknesses in profitability and growth. Revenue and earnings have been inconsistent, making it hard to predict where this business will be in a few years. Dangerous-rated stocks can still recover, but they carry a higher risk of permanent capital loss and should only be held with a clear thesis and strict position sizing.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Gold Royalty Corp. demonstrates weak fundamental quality and is constrained by profitability. Key risks to watch: a weak near-term growth outlook; inconsistent earnings; balance sheet constraints on growth. The stock trades at a 33% premium to estimated intrinsic value and is warrants caution — careful position sizing and ongoing monitoring are essential.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Gold Royalty Corp. demonstrates weak fundamental quality and is constrained by profitability.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: a weak near-term growth outlook; inconsistent earnings; balance sheet constraints on growth.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: The stock trades at a 33% premium to estimated intrinsic value and is warrants caution — careful position sizing and ongoing monitoring are essential.

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

forecast_meta.constraint_note: EPS growth moderated from 0.0% to 2.0% due to financial strength score of 6/10
forecast_meta.growth_explanation: Base (2.0%/yr): growth in line with analyst expectations. Bull (2.6%/yr): everything goes to plan. Bear (1.1%/yr): growth disappoints. Growth capped at 2.0% (from 0.0%) — the balance sheet (financial strength 6/10) limits how fast this company can realistically grow.

bull_points: Revenue growing at 34.2% year-over-year, indicating sustained demand momentum heading into the next quarter
Current market positioning in the Basic Materials sector may attract tactical buyers
Any positive earnings surprise could act as a near-term re-rating catalyst
bear_points: Trading at a 33% premium to intrinsic value — elevated risk of near-term multiple compression
Low consensus EPS growth forecast of 0.0% p.a. limits re-rating potential in the near term
Short-term earnings visibility is limited without additional guidance

bull_points: Gold Royalty Corp. operates in a sector with long-term structural tailwinds
Management has demonstrated consistent capital allocation discipline
The business has the scale to compound earnings over a multi-year horizon
bear_points: Low profitability score (0/10) suggests thin or negative margins that structurally limit long-term earnings power
ROIC of 0.17% is below most cost-of-capital benchmarks, suggesting the business destroys value over the long run
Unpredictable earnings (2/10) make long-term forecasting unreliable and compress the valuation multiple the market is willing to pay

watchlist_signal.reason: Weak fundamentals or significantly overvalued — better opportunities elsewhere.

company_profile.name: Gran Tierra Energy Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (energy) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Gran Tierra Energy Inc., together with its subsidiaries, is involved in the exploration and production of oil and gas properties in Colombia, Canada, and Ecuador. The company has a strategic partnership with Ecopetrol S.A. for the development of fields in the Middle Magdalena Valley. The company was founded in 2003 and is headquartered in Calgary, Canada.
company_profile.description_summary: Gran Tierra Energy Inc., together with its subsidiaries, is involved in the exploration and production of oil and gas properties in Colombia, Canada, and Ecuador. The company has a strategic partnership with Ecopetrol S.A.
